# Core Patent Statute — Title 35 U.S.C. (Selected Sections)

> **Public-domain notice.** The text and substance below are drawn from Title 35 of the
> United States Code. As a work of the United States Government, the U.S. Code is in the
> **public domain** (17 U.S.C. § 105) and is free to reproduce. Section text for the short
> "core" provisions (§§ 101, 102, 103, 112) is quoted verbatim; longer/procedural sections
> are summarized faithfully with a link out to the authoritative source.
>

## § 101 — Inventions patentable (eligibility)

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/101

> Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or
> composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent
> therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title.

*Gloss:* Defines the four statutory categories of patent-eligible subject matter — process,
machine, manufacture, composition of matter (plus improvements) — and requires that the claimed
invention be both **new** and **useful**. (Judicial exceptions — abstract ideas, laws of nature,
natural phenomena — are layered on top by case law, not by this text.)

---

## § 102 — Conditions for patentability; novelty / statutory bars

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/102

**(a) Novelty; Prior Art.** A person shall be entitled to a patent unless —

> **(1)** the claimed invention was patented, described in a printed publication, or in public
> use, on sale, or otherwise available to the public before the effective filing date of the
> claimed invention; or
>
> **(2)** the claimed invention was described in a patent issued under section 151, or in an
> application for patent published or deemed published under section 122(b), in which the
> patent or application, as the case may be, names another inventor and was effectively filed
> before the effective filing date of the claimed invention.

**(b) Exceptions.**

> **(1) Disclosures made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of the claimed
> invention.** A disclosure made 1 year or less before the effective filing date of a claimed
> invention shall not be prior art to the claimed invention under subsection (a)(1) if —
> (A) the disclosure was made by the inventor or joint inventor or by another who obtained the
> subject matter disclosed directly or indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor; or
> (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such disclosure, been publicly disclosed by the
> inventor or a joint inventor or another who obtained the subject matter disclosed directly or
> indirectly from the inventor or a joint inventor.
>
> **(2) Disclosures appearing in applications and patents.** A disclosure shall not be prior art
> to a claimed invention under subsection (a)(2) if —
> (A) the subject matter disclosed was obtained directly or indirectly from the inventor or a
> joint inventor; (B) the subject matter disclosed had, before such subject matter was
> effectively filed under subsection (a)(2), been publicly disclosed by the inventor or a joint
> inventor or another who obtained the subject matter directly or indirectly from the inventor
> or a joint inventor; or (C) the subject matter disclosed and the claimed invention, not later
> than the effective filing date of the claimed invention, were owned by the same person or
> subject to an obligation of assignment to the same person.

**(c) Common ownership under joint research agreements.** Subject matter and a claimed invention
are treated as commonly owned under (b)(2)(C) where the subject matter was developed and the
claimed invention was made under a joint research agreement in effect on or before the effective
filing date, the claimed invention resulted from activities within the scope of that agreement,
and the application discloses (or is amended to disclose) the parties to the agreement.

**(d) Patents and published applications effective as prior art.** A patent or published
application is deemed effectively filed, as to subject matter described in it, as of (1) its
actual filing date, or (2) if entitled to claim priority/benefit, the filing date of the
earliest such application that describes the subject matter.

*Gloss:* The post-AIA **first-inventor-to-file** novelty and statutory-bar framework: anything
publicly available before the effective filing date is prior art (§ 102(a)(1)), as is an earlier-
filed but later-published patent/application of another (§ 102(a)(2)), subject to a 1-year
inventor-origin grace period and common-ownership exceptions (§ 102(b)).

---

## § 103 — Conditions for patentability; non-obvious subject matter

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/103

> A patent for a claimed invention may not be obtained, notwithstanding that the claimed
> invention is not identically disclosed as set forth in section 102, if the differences between
> the claimed invention and the prior art are such that the claimed invention as a whole would
> have been obvious before the effective filing date of the claimed invention to a person having
> ordinary skill in the art to which the claimed invention pertains. Patentability shall not be
> negated by the manner in which the invention was made.

*Gloss:* The **non-obviousness** bar — even if no single reference anticipates, a claim is
unpatentable if the differences from the prior art would have been obvious "as a whole" to a
person of ordinary skill in the art (PHOSITA). The *Graham v. John Deere* factual inquiries
(scope/content of prior art, differences, level of skill, secondary considerations) implement
this test.

---

## § 112 — Specification (written description, enablement, definiteness, means-plus-function)

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/112

**(a) In general.**

> The specification shall contain a written description of the invention, and of the manner and
> process of making and using it, in such full, clear, concise, and exact terms as to enable any
> person skilled in the art to which it pertains, or with which it is most nearly connected, to
> make and use the same, and shall set forth the best mode contemplated by the inventor or joint
> inventor of carrying out the invention.

**(b) Conclusion.**

> The specification shall conclude with one or more claims particularly pointing out and
> distinctly claiming the subject matter which the inventor or a joint inventor regards as the
> invention.

**(c) Form.**

> A claim may be written in independent or, if the nature of the case admits, in dependent or
> multiple dependent form.

**(d) Reference in dependent forms.** A dependent claim shall contain a reference to a claim
previously set forth and then specify a further limitation of the subject matter claimed; it
shall be construed to incorporate by reference all the limitations of the claim to which it
refers.

**(e) Reference in multiple dependent form.** A multiple dependent claim shall refer in the
alternative to more than one previously set forth claim, may not serve as a basis for any other
multiple dependent claim, and shall be construed to incorporate all the limitations of the
particular claim in relation to which it is being considered.

**(f) Element in claim for a combination.**

> An element in a claim for a combination may be expressed as a means or step for performing a
> specified function without the recital of structure, material, or acts in support thereof, and
> such claim shall be construed to cover the corresponding structure, material, or acts described
> in the specification and equivalents thereof.

*Gloss:* § 112(a) imposes three disclosure requirements — **written description**, **enablement**,
and **best mode** (best-mode failure is no longer a defense to validity per AIA § 15, but the
duty remains); § 112(b) is the **definiteness** requirement (claims must inform a PHOSITA of the
scope with reasonable certainty — *Nautilus*); § 112(f) governs **means-plus-function** claiming,
construed to cover only the corresponding structure disclosed in the specification and its
equivalents.

---

## § 115 — Inventor's oath or declaration

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/115

**Operative requirement (summary).** Each individual who is the inventor or a joint inventor of
a claimed invention in an application must execute an **oath or declaration** in connection with
the application. Under subsection (b), the oath/declaration must state that (1) the application
was made or was authorized to be made by the affiant or declarant, and (2) such individual
**believes himself or herself to be the original inventor or an original joint inventor** of a
claimed invention in the application.

**Substitute statement (subsection (d)).** A **substitute statement** may be filed in lieu of an
oath/declaration where the individual is **deceased, under legal incapacity, or cannot be found
or reached after diligent effort**, or is **under an obligation to assign but has refused** to
execute the oath/declaration. The substitute statement must identify the individual, the
circumstances permitting its use, and any additional information the Director requires.

**Deadline.** A required oath, declaration, substitute statement, or recorded assignment
containing the required statements may be postponed but must be provided **no later than the
date on which the issue fee is paid**.

---

## § 116 — Inventors / joint inventors

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/116

**Operative requirement (summary).** When an invention is made by two or more persons jointly,
they **apply for patent jointly** and each makes the required oath, except as otherwise provided.

**Subsection (a) — flexibility of joint invention.** Inventors may apply jointly even though
(1) they did not **physically work together or at the same time**, (2) each did not make the
**same type or amount** of contribution, or (3) each did not contribute to the subject matter of
**every claim**.

**Subsection (b) — omitted/uncooperative joint inventor.** If a joint inventor refuses to join
or cannot be found or reached after diligent effort, the application may be made by the other
inventor(s) on behalf of the omitted inventor.

**Subsection (c) — correction of error.** Where, **through error**, a person is named as an
inventor or an inventor is not named, the Director may permit the application to be amended to
correct the inventorship.

---

## § 171 — Patents for designs

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/171

**(a) In general (quoted).**

> Whoever invents any new, original and ornamental design for an article of manufacture may
> obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title.

**Operative requirement (summary).** A design patent protects a design that is **new, original,
and ornamental** as applied to an **article of manufacture** (function-driven/non-ornamental
designs are not protectable). Under **subsection (b)**, the provisions of Title 35 relating to
patents for inventions apply to design patents except as otherwise provided. (Term: design
patents granted on applications filed on or after May 13, 2015 run **15 years from grant** —
see 35 U.S.C. § 173.)

---

## § 119 — Benefit of earlier filing date; right of priority

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/119

**Subsection (a) — foreign priority.** A U.S. application for a like invention is entitled to
the **same effect** (effective filing date) as the earlier **foreign** application if filed
**within 12 months** of the earliest foreign filing, provided the foreign country grants similar
priority rights to U.S. applicants or is a WTO member. The Director may extend the 12-month
window by **2 months** for an **unintentional** delay, upon payment of the prescribed fee.
(For design applications the window is 6 months — § 172.)

**Subsection (e) — provisional application benefit.** A later non-provisional (or international
application designating the U.S.) gets the benefit of a **provisional** application's filing
date if: (1) the provisional discloses the invention as required by § 112(a) (other than best
mode); (2) the non-provisional is filed **within 12 months** of the provisional (extendable
2 months for unintentional delay); and (3) it contains or is amended to contain a **specific
reference** to the provisional.

---

## § 120 — Benefit of earlier filing date in the United States

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/120

**Operative requirement (summary).** A continuing U.S. application (continuation, divisional, or
continuation-in-part) is entitled to the **earlier U.S. application's filing date** for commonly
disclosed subject matter if **all** of the following are met:

1. **Disclosure** — the invention is disclosed in the earlier application in the manner provided
   by **§ 112(a)** (other than the best-mode requirement);
2. **Common inventor** — the later application names **an inventor or joint inventor** named in
   the earlier application;
3. **Co-pendency** — the later application is filed **before the patenting, abandonment, or
   termination of proceedings** on the earlier application; and
4. **Specific reference** — the later application contains (or is amended during pendency to
   contain) a **specific reference** to the earlier application.

Failure to timely submit the specific reference may be treated as a waiver, subject to acceptance
of an unintentionally delayed submission upon payment of the prescribed fee.

---

## § 184 — Filing of application in foreign country (foreign filing license)

**Source:** https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/184

**Operative requirement (summary).** Except as authorized by a **license** from the Commissioner
(Director), a person **shall not file (or cause/authorize to be filed) in any foreign country**
an application on an invention made in the United States until **6 months after** the
corresponding U.S. filing — and may not, before that period or before a license is granted,
modify, amend, or supplement a foreign filing in a way that alters the U.S. application beyond
what was licensed.

- **6-month rule.** Foreign filing is barred for 6 months after U.S. filing unless a foreign
  filing license is granted earlier.
- **License grant / deemed grant.** In practice the USPTO acts on the request promptly; a foreign
  filing license is commonly indicated on the official filing receipt. The statute also provides
  for a **retroactive license** where the foreign filing occurred **through error**, the
  application does **not** disclose an invention under a secrecy order (§ 181), and the error was
  without deceptive intent.

*Note:* The Cornell fetch confirmed the prohibition, the 6-month rule, and the retroactive-license-
for-error provision. The "deemed granted / promptly indicated on filing receipt" practice detail
is implementing USPTO practice (37 CFR 5.11–5.15), not § 184 text itself.
